expectations.text: The response shows at least one complete red-green cycle (test fails, code written, test passes)
expectations.evidence: Cycle 1 shows a failing test (`ModuleNotFoundError` after `pytest -q`), then adds `password_validator.py` with `is_valid_password`, then reruns tests with `1 passed`. The same fail-then-fix-then-pass structure is repeated in Cycles 2-5.

expectations.text: Tests are written one at a time, not all upfront before any implementation
expectations.evidence: The response proceeds in numbered cycles and introduces one new test per cycle: under-8 chars, missing uppercase, missing lowercase, missing digit, then valid password acceptance. Code changes follow each newly added failing test.

expectations.text: Test names describe behavior (e.g., 'rejects', 'accepts', 'requires'), not implementation
The implementation for each test is minimal — only enough to pass the current test
expectations.evidence: Each green step adds only the missing rule for that cycle. In Cycle 4 the function intentionally ends with `return False` and explains this is deliberate until the acceptance test exists, then Cycle 5 changes it to `return True` after the acceptance test fails.

expectations.text: Tests use the public function interface, not internal state
expectations.evidence: All tests import and call only `is_valid_password` from `password_validator` and assert on its return value; no internal variables or private helpers are accessed.

uncertainties: The run presents expected `pytest` outputs as simulated narrative examples rather than captured command execution logs, so timing and tool telemetry are not available.
workarounds: Graded assertions against the transcript and final response content and used local convention defaults for missing runtime telemetry fields.

suggestions: Record actual test execution output in future runs to make red/green evidence and runtime metrics directly verifiable.
eval_feedback.overall: This run meets all TDD assertions with clear cycle-by-cycle evidence in the response.
